next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

By the time Marisol reached Flatbush, the hems of her jeans were dark with rain and bus salt. The corner bodega had sold out of marigolds, so she brought two black candles, a loaf of Portuguese bread wrapped in paper gone translucent with butter, and a plastic bag of limes she had not planned to buy. She stood in the hallway outside her mother’s apartment and listened before knocking: the TV low, Leo arguing with somebody in Minecraft, Gloria’s voice from the kitchen saying somebody better not touch that pot with a wet spoon.

When Carina opened the door she had a phone pinned between shoulder and jaw, one sneaker half unlaced, and a stack of school forms under her arm with fluorescent tabs sprouting from the top like feathers. She looked Marisol up and down and stepped aside without hanging up.

“Yeah, Ms. Perez, I hear you. No, I didn’t lose it, they changed the portal again. Hold on, my sister’s here looking like a drenched pigeon.”

Marisol kissed her cheek anyway. “I brought candles.”

“Good, because Mami bought the saint kind and they smell like old man cologne.” Carina pointed the forms at the kitchen. “Put your wet coat on the chair, not the sofa. Leo, move your backpack before your aunt trips and sues us.”

The apartment held the same blended weather it always did in late fall: frying garlic, wet wool, laundry soap, and the honey-wax perfume of prayer candles burning by Tomas’s photo in the corner. Gloria stood at the stove in a faded Mercy Hospital sweatshirt and an apron that said KISS THE COOK in glittering letters worn down to KISS THE C. Her hair...
open_hooks_to_carry: Interview room pressure comes from silence and interrupted speech, not action set pieces.
Salvador says the family has been painting halos over burns.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...vision, still there, still offering the exact fast mercy she had trained herself not to call mercy. She turned away from it and walked back inside, shoes squeaking on the service corridor linoleum.

At a staff desk outside Administration, she opened her laptop and drafted the email while her pulse still ran high enough to keep her honest.

Subject: Interview Protocol Confirmation - Rivas

Nora,

Confirming that I accept the three-session closed-door structure requested by Salvador Rivas, with scheduling routed through your office and contemporaneous notes submitted after each session. I understand this deviates from standard witness protocol and carries dispute risk. Please send addendum language for signature today.

Thanks,
Marisol Vega

She read it twice, deleted one adjective, sent it.

Then she opened a new document titled MARTYR MANUSCRIPT - FACT LOG and pasted the notes from her notebook, line by line, before memory could sand them smooth. She added a final entry:

Constraint accepted: access over procedural safety.

When she leaned back, the museum hummed around her, carts rolling, printers spitting labels, a child somewhere in the lobby asking too loudly whether dead artists still got paid. Through the window she could see rain stippling the plaza and, beyond it, the city moving with complete indifference to whatever she had just agreed to. Her hands still shook, but the shake had changed. Not appetite now. Aftershock.

Thirty months sober, she reminded herself, pressing her thumb against the ridge of her burn scar until the skin hurt.

Thirty months, and now this.
state_deltas_required: Test whether Salvador's first claims are credible enough to pursue.
Marisol decides to investigate the contradiction instead of dismissing it, and she tells sponsor Luis before craving escalates.
